questions.q: A 5-node etcd cluster loses 3 members simultaneously. What happens to the cluster?
options: The cluster continues operating with the 2 remaining nodes
The cluster becomes read-only until a node is restored
The cluster loses quorum (needs 3) and stops accepting writes
The cluster automatically elects a new leader from the 2 remaining nodes
questions.explanation: A 5-node etcd cluster requires a quorum of ⌊5/2⌋+1 = 3 nodes to commit writes. With only 2 remaining, quorum is lost and the cluster cannot process write requests, preventing split-brain data divergence.

questions.q: Why is it always recommended to use an odd number of etcd nodes (e.g., 3 or 5) rather than an even number (e.g., 4)?
options: Even numbers cause etcd to use more CPU during leader elections
4 nodes gives the same fault tolerance as 3 (can only lose 1) but has more risk in split-brain without any benefit
Even numbers of nodes cannot form a valid Raft cluster
Kubernetes only supports odd-numbered etcd topologies via kubeadm
questions.explanation: A 4-node cluster requires quorum of 3 (can only tolerate losing 1 node) — the same as a 3-node cluster. But 4 nodes cost more and introduce split-brain risk (2 vs 2 partition). Always prefer 3 or 5 to maximise fault tolerance per node.

questions.q: Which etcdctl command is used to create a point-in-time backup of the etcd data store?
questions.explanation: The ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save <path> command creates a consistent snapshot of the etcd database. You must provide the --endpoints, --cacert, --cert, and --key flags for authentication.
